questions.body: What is the prevalence of primary adrenal insufficiency?
questions.ideal_answer: The prevalence of primary adrenal insufficiency is estimated at between 82 and 144 cases per million people. Auto-immunity is the most common cause in adults, while genetic causes, especially enzyme defects, are the most common cause in children. By comparison, secondary adrenal insufficiency has a higher estimated prevalence of 150-280 per million. Acute adrenal crisis occurs at an incidence of approximately 6-8 per 100 patients per year in Europe, with a prospective study reporting 0.5 deaths per 100 patient-years from adrenal crisis. Long-term morbidity includes alterations in quality of life in both physical and mental health components, though increased cardiovascular risk and bone demineralization are not consistently reported across studies.

questions.body: What is adductor canal syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: Adductor canal syndrome is a rare non-atherosclerotic vascular disorder in which the superficial femoral artery is externally compressed within the adductor canal, typically by an anomalous musculotendinous band or hypertrophic surrounding musculature. It predominantly affects young, athletic individuals subjected to repetitive physical stress. The compression can lead to arterial occlusion, thrombosis, and critical lower limb ischemia. Symptoms include claudication, pain, paresthesia, pallor, and absent pedal pulses. Diagnosis is frequently delayed due to its rarity and symptom overlap with other common causes of leg pain in athletes.

questions.body: What are the main types of DNA damage induced by UV radiation?
questions.ideal_answer: UV radiation induces several main types of DNA damage. The most well-characterized are cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers (CPDs), specifically thymine-thymine cyclobutane dimers (T-T), formed by covalent linkage between adjacent thymine bases. A second major photoproduct is the thymine-thymine pyrimidine-pyrimidone (6-4) photoproduct (T(6-4)T), which is mutagenic and continues to accumulate with increasing UV irradiation. Both lesions arise from intrastrand DNA cross-links, though interstrand cross-links can also occur. UV exposure additionally causes DNA strand breaks, which serve as early intermediates in the DNA repair process but can severely impair cellular activities if left unrepaired. Sister chromatid exchange and more general DNA breakage have also been documented, particularly in preimplantation embryos exposed to UV light. Together, these lesions inhibit DNA replication and, if unrepaired, increase the risk of mutagenesis and genomic instability.

questions.body: What are indications of quetiapine?
questions.ideal_answer: Quetiapine is an atypical antipsychotic with several approved and off-label indications. Its approved indications include schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, generalized anxiety disorder, severe depression, dementia, and mood disorders. It is also indicated for multiple drug abuse (MDA) and psychosis. Studies further demonstrate its off-label use for organic mental disorders, disorders due to psychoactive substance use, and affective disorders. In one retrospective study, MDA was the most frequent indication (46.2%), followed by psychosis (29.7%) and depression (24.1%), with MDA patients tending to be younger males receiving lower doses. A large German multicenter study identified quetiapine as one of the most commonly prescribed off-label psychotropic drugs, particularly for organic mental disorders and disorders due to psychoactive substance use.
exact_answer: disorders due to psychoactive substance use

questions.body: In GBM, which cell state is associated with the loss of Neurofibromin (NF1)?
questions.ideal_answer: In GBM, loss of Neurofibromin (NF1) is strongly associated with the mesenchymal cell state. Multiple lines of evidence support this: NF1 regulates mesenchymal GBM plasticity and aggressiveness through the AP-1 transcription factor FOSL1, and NF1-mutant BTSCs display a mesenchymal gene signature that is lost upon FOSL1 depletion. NF1 loss in patient-derived glioma cells promotes mesenchymal subtype and epithelial-mesenchymal transition-specific gene expression, increased self-renewal, and heightened invasion. Genomic analyses show that NF1 genetic loss in GBM is usually associated with the mesenchymal molecular subtype, and tumors with NF1 loss frequently express the mesenchymal marker podoplanin. Single-nucleus RNA sequencing of NF1-mutant GBMs further demonstrates that mesenchymal-like (MES-like) tumor cells are enriched for MEK activation signatures, and MEK inhibition selectively depletes these MES-like cells. Collectively, these findings establish a strong link between NF1 loss and the mesenchymal cell state in GBM.

questions.body: What the recurrence percentage for Cushing's Disease?
questions.ideal_answer: Recurrence rates for Cushing's disease following transsphenoidal surgery vary widely across studies, ranging from 3% to 46%, depending on definition criteria and duration of follow-up. Most reviews cite recurrence rates of 15% to 25%, while published rates of 5% to 20% are also commonly reported, with late recurrences occurring up to 20 years post-surgery. One recent center of excellence study reported a recurrence rate of 27.4% after a median follow-up of 38 months. About half of recurrences appear within 5 years of surgery and the remaining half within or even after 10 years. Factors associated with higher recurrence include macroadenomas compared to microadenomas (45% vs 19%), while immediate post-operative cortisol insufficiency and longer glucocorticoid replacement duration are protective. Because recurrence can occur decades after initial treatment, lifelong follow-up with annual clinical and hormonal assessments is recommended, with late-night salivary cortisol considered among the earliest and most sensitive markers for detecting recurrence.

questions.body: What causes neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses (NCLs)?
questions.ideal_answer: Neuronal ceroid lipofuscinoses (NCLs) are rare, inherited lysosomal storage disorders caused by aberrant lysosomal function. They are characterized by the accumulation of autofluorescent ceroid lipopigment, leading to progressive neurodegeneration. NCLs encompass various types (CLN1 to CLN14, excluding CLN9), each linked to a particular mutated gene. For example, CLN7 and CLN8 are caused by pathogenic mutations confirmed by whole exome sequencing, CLN2 involves deficiency of a lysosomal enzyme, and CLN11 is associated with homozygous pathogenic mutations in the progranulin gene (GRN). Mitochondrial impairment, including dysfunctional oxidative phosphorylation and altered mitochondrial membrane potential, has also been implicated in disease pathogenesis. The resulting clinical features include progressive cognitive decline, epilepsy, motor dysfunction, and vision loss, with symptom onset and severity varying by subtype.

questions.body: Does the PI4KIIalpha of Drosophila melanogaster show homology with that of Homo sapiens?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, PI4KIIalpha of Drosophila melanogaster shows homology with that of Homo sapiens. Abstract 3 explicitly describes this novel class of phosphatidylinositol 4-kinases as highly conserved evolutionarily from yeast to humans, indicating that the PI4KII family, including PI4KIIalpha, is present across a broad range of species including both Drosophila and humans. Abstract 1 further supports this by characterizing both rat PI4KIIalpha and Drosophila PI4KII as members of the same enzyme family, demonstrating that the catalytic domain and functional properties are shared. Although PI4KIIs bear essentially no sequence similarity to other protein or lipid kinases and represent a distinct branch of the kinase superfamily, they are conserved within their own family across species. The depletion of PI4KII in Drosophila cells caused a severe reduction in PtdIns 4-kinase activity, underscoring the in vivo importance of this conserved enzyme, and confirming that the Drosophila and human PI4KIIalpha enzymes are homologous members of the same evolutionarily conserved kinase family.

questions.body: Vitamin deficiencies in severe alcoholism.
questions.ideal_answer: Severe alcoholism is strongly associated with several vitamin deficiencies, primarily due to malnutrition, poor dietary intake, and the direct metabolic effects of chronic ethanol exposure. Thiamine (vitamin B1) deficiency is the most common and clinically significant, leading to conditions such as Wernicke's encephalopathy, Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome, and beriberi (both dry beriberi with ascending paralysis and wet beriberi with cardiac involvement, including beriberi heart disease). Chronic alcohol abuse is the leading risk factor for thiamine deficiency. Additionally, alcoholic liver disease is closely linked to deficiencies in folate, vitamin B-6, and vitamin B-12, all of which are essential for hepatic methionine metabolism. Disruption of this metabolic pathway by ethanol, compounded by these vitamin deficiencies, contributes significantly to the pathogenesis of alcoholic liver disease and related complications. Recognizing and promptly treating these deficiencies, particularly through thiamine supplementation, is critical in the management of patients with severe alcoholism.

questions.body: Can tumor cells be reprogrammed into immune cells?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, tumor cells can be reprogrammed into immune cells. Research has demonstrated that tumor cells can be converted in vivo into type 1 conventional dendritic cells through adenoviral delivery of the transcription factors PU.1, IRF8, and BATF3. These reprogrammed tumor cells gained the ability to present antigens, remodel the tumor microenvironment, recruit and expand polyclonal cytotoxic T cells, induce tumor regressions, and establish long-term systemic immunity in multiple mouse melanoma models. Importantly, in human tumor spheroids and xenografts, this reprogramming progressed independently of immunosuppression, which commonly limits conventional immunotherapy. These findings pave the way for human clinical trials of in vivo immune cell reprogramming as a cancer immunotherapy strategy.

questions.body: What are the typically biomedical characteristics of primary adrenal insufficiency?
questions.ideal_answer: Primary adrenal insufficiency is biomedically characterized by a deficiency of all adrenocortical hormones, distinguishing it from secondary and glucocorticoid-induced forms. Diagnostically, it typically presents with low early-morning serum cortisol levels (specifically less than 5 µg/dL), elevated corticotropin (ACTH) levels reflecting the pituitary's attempt to stimulate the failing adrenal glands, and low dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate (DHEAS) levels indicating impaired adrenal androgen production. These hormonal profiles, measured via early-morning blood tests, differentiate primary adrenal insufficiency from secondary and glucocorticoid-induced adrenal insufficiency, which typically show low or intermediate cortisol with low or low-normal corticotropin and DHEAS levels.

questions.body: Please list the common clinical characteristic of the Heidenhain variant of Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease (HvCJD)
questions.ideal_answer: The hallmark clinical characteristic of the Heidenhain variant of Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (HvCJD) is the early and prominent onset of visual symptoms, which typically precede other neurological manifestations by weeks. The most common visual disturbance is blurred vision, followed by progressive loss of visual acuity, declining visual fields, and homonymic hemianopsia. Patients may also experience spells of visual dysfunction, visual hallucinations, and, in genetic cases, cortical blindness. As the disease progresses, additional neurological features emerge, including ataxia, memory impairment, paranoia, aggression, and broader behavioral changes reflecting rapid cognitive decline. Seizures can occur but are uncommon, reported in only approximately 5.5% of cases. The disease is rapidly progressive and fatal, with death often occurring within weeks to months of diagnosis. The distinguishing feature of HvCJD from other CJD variants is this initial predominance of visual symptoms before cognitive impairment becomes apparent.

questions.body: Does whole-chromosome silencing occur in somatic cells?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, whole-chromosome silencing occurs in somatic cells through a process known as X chromosome inactivation (XCI). In female mammals, one of the two X chromosomes is largely silenced to achieve dosage compensation, and this silencing is maintained in somatic tissues throughout life. The long non-coding RNA XIST plays a central role, coating the inactive X and recruiting repressive complexes; notably, XIST is continuously required in adult human B cells to silence X-linked immune genes such as TLR7 via histone deacetylation. Somatic cell hybrid studies confirm that genes like Zfx, Rps4, and Ube1x are expressed exclusively from the active X, while Xist is expressed only from the inactive X. XCI creates a mosaic pattern in somatic tissues, as illustrated by mice carrying Piga mutations and by cases of Duchenne muscular dystrophy in females with skewed inactivation. Inactivation can even spread into adjacent autosomal material in X;autosome translocations, though incompletely. Aberrant XCI patterns in cloned cattle further demonstrate the importance of proper epigenetic regulation of whole-chromosome silencing in somatic cells.

questions.body: Are eating disorders common in male adolescents?
questions.ideal_answer: Eating disorders are significantly less common in male adolescents compared to females, but they do occur. The Canadian hospitalization data shows male rates of 5.9 per 100,000 versus 66.7 per 100,000 in females. The Sarawak study found a disordered eating prevalence of 15.6% in males compared to 28.6% in females. The Spanish data confirms female predominance for eating disorders among hospitalized adolescents. Despite being less frequent, eating disorders in males should not be overlooked, as the associated factors such as perceived sociocultural pressure, drive for muscularity, and perfectionism are shared across genders, indicating that males remain a vulnerable population requiring targeted prevention and intervention strategies.

questions.body: What is the estimated incidence of endogenous Cushing's Syndrome in the human population?
questions.ideal_answer: The estimated incidence of endogenous Cushing's Syndrome ranges from 2 to 8 per million people annually. A second source specifies the incidence more narrowly at 2 to 4 cases per million inhabitants per year. Cushing's disease, caused by a corticotropin-secreting pituitary adenoma and representing 60% to 70% of endogenous cases, has an estimated incidence of 1.2 to 2.4 cases per million inhabitants per year and occurs 3 to 8 times more frequently in women than in men. ACTH-independent Cushing's syndrome accounts for approximately 20% of endogenous cases, most commonly due to adrenal adenomas (10%) or adrenal carcinomas (8%). Bilateral micronodular and macronodular adrenal hyperplasia are less frequent causes. Overall, endogenous Cushing's Syndrome is considered a rare condition, and cases due to ectopic ACTH secretion are thought to be underdiagnosed.
exact_answer: 2-4 cases per million inhabitants per year
1.2 to 2.4 cases per million inhabitants per year

questions.body: Please list the types of medication errors
questions.ideal_answer: Medication errors encompass a wide range of types across various healthcare settings. These include delivery to the incorrect patient and privacy breaches in community pharmacy home delivery. In pediatric emergency departments, the most common types are dosing errors and wrong or improper drugs, with errors also arising from self-medication and wrong administration by family members. In intensive care units, the most frequent errors are wrong infusion rate, unauthorized medication, and wrong time errors. Among nursing students, documentation errors and dose errors are most common. Across hospital and community settings, particularly in pediatrics, wrong dose, wrong technique, and wrong route errors are frequently reported, along with near-misses. Contributing factors include medication knowledge deficits, deviation from procedures, lack of patient information, staffing issues, environmental distractions, and insufficient training.
exact_answer: wrong administration of medication by family members

questions.body: What is the most common genotoxic factor that humans are exposed to?
questions.ideal_answer: The most common genotoxic factor humans are exposed to is solar UV radiation. Solar ultraviolet radiation is explicitly identified as a major environmental genotoxic factor and a primary contributor to skin carcinogenesis. UV radiation, particularly UVB (280-320 nm), directly induces DNA damage by forming cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers (CPDs) and pyrimidine (6-4) pyrimidone photoproducts. UVA (320-400 nm) also contributes to genotoxicity, largely through the generation of reactive oxygen species such as singlet oxygen, which leads to oxidative DNA damage including 8-oxoguanine formation. Additionally, UV radiation promotes photocarcinogenesis through immunosuppression and inflammation. Solar radiation is Earth's predominant natural source of UV radiation, making it the most ubiquitous genotoxic exposure for humans and other organisms. Prolonged exposure contributes to photoaging, skin cancer, and melanoma development. While visible light has also been shown to contribute to oxidative photocarcinogenesis, UV radiation remains the dominant genotoxic component of solar radiation consistently highlighted across the literature.

questions.body: What are the recommended screening tests to diagnose the presence of Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: The recommended screening tests to diagnose Cushing's Syndrome, as outlined by the Endocrine Society's Clinical Practice Guidelines, are the 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (DST), late-night salivary cortisol (LNSC), and 24-hour urinary free cortisol (UFC). The 1 mg DST assesses the normal feedback reaction of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis; a morning serum cortisol below 50 nmol/L can exclude Cushing's syndrome unless clinical suspicion is high. Late-night salivary cortisol exploits the fact that the normal circadian nadir of cortisol is lost in Cushing's syndrome, resulting in elevated nighttime values that are conveniently measurable in saliva. Urinary free cortisol reflects integrated tissue exposure to free cortisol over 24 hours and is particularly useful in severe hypercortisolism. Guidelines recommend selecting a single test with high diagnostic accuracy tailored to the individual patient's characteristics and lifestyle, as each test has specific procedural limitations and potential for false-positive or false-negative results.

questions.body: Is Seborrhea a condition that affects the gastrointestinal tract?
questions.ideal_answer: Seborrhea (or seborrheic dermatitis) is not a condition that affects the gastrointestinal tract. As described in the provided literature, seborrheic dermatitis is a common, chronic inflammatory skin condition that affects sebaceous gland-rich areas of the body, such as the scalp, face, and body folds. Its multifactorial etiology involves sebocyte activity, skin microbiome dysbiosis, and immune factors operating within the skin. Seborrhea, in the context of oily skin, refers to excess sebum production on the skin surface. Treatments are directed at the skin through topical antifungals, corticosteroids, calcineurin inhibitors, and systemic agents. None of the available evidence suggests any involvement of the gastrointestinal tract in this condition.

questions.body: What impact could biosynthetic meat have on human health?
questions.ideal_answer: Cultured meat presents both potential health benefits and uncertainties. On the positive side, it allows compositional optimization, such as improved omega-3 to omega-6 fatty acid ratios, reduced pathogen exposure, and lower risk of antimicrobial resistance compared to conventional meat. It may also reduce chronic disease risks associated with red and processed meat consumption. However, cultured meat may be deficient in key nutrients like vitamin B12, heme iron, creatine, and taurine unless specifically fortified. Critically, no clinical trials have yet evaluated its effects on human health, and potential nutritional, biological, and chemical risks remain insufficiently characterized, making definitive health conclusions premature.

questions.body: What are common blood cell changes caused by elevated cortisol levels?
questions.ideal_answer: Elevated cortisol levels, as seen in Cushing's syndrome, cause several characteristic blood cell changes. The most common findings include leukocytosis (increased total white blood cell count), neutrophilia (elevated neutrophil counts), and an elevated neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR). Conversely, cortisol suppresses lymphocyte counts, leading to relative lymphopenia, and reduces eosinophil counts. Studies in both adult and pediatric Cushing's syndrome patients also demonstrate alterations in the platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR) and monocyte-to-lymphocyte ratio (MLR). These immune cell changes correlate with the degree of hypercortisolism and are observed across different Cushing's syndrome subtypes, though specific patterns may vary. Relative lymphopenia is a particularly frequent finding with high sensitivity for Cushing's syndrome, while leukocytosis and neutrophilia are also common. These changes partially reverse after surgical remission, though white blood cell counts may not fully normalize, and baseline leukocytosis predicts a poorer remission prognosis.

questions.body: is Rheumatic heart disease caused by Staphylococcus Aureus?
questions.ideal_answer: Rheumatic heart disease (RHD) is not caused by Staphylococcus aureus. RHD is a complication of acute rheumatic fever (ARF), which is triggered by an abnormal autoimmune response to Group A Streptococcus (GAS) infection. Through a process of molecular mimicry, the immune system targets host tissues after a GAS infection, leading to inflammation and damage to the heart valves, joints, and nervous system. RHD is the leading cause of valvular heart disease globally and results specifically from this post-infection immune response to GAS, not Staphylococcus aureus. Research efforts, including the development of a 30-valent M protein-based vaccine targeting Group A Streptococcus, further confirm that GAS is the causative organism underlying ARF and RHD.

questions.body: In which organisms is a PI4KIIalpha (PI4KIIα) orthologue present?
questions.ideal_answer: PI4KIIalpha (PI4KIIα) orthologues have been identified and studied in a range of organisms. In humans, PI4K2A deficiency causes neurodevelopmental and epileptic-dyskinetic encephalopathy. Mouse models have been used to study PI4K2A's role in autophagic lysosome reformation and hereditary spastic paraplegia. The rat PI4KIIalpha isoform was used to define the minimal catalytic domain of the enzyme. Bovine mammary epithelial cells express PI4K2A, where it positively regulates lipid synthesis. Pekin ducks have a PI4K2A gene involved in lipid homeostasis during viral infection. Drosophila PI4KII has been expressed and characterized, with depletion causing severe reduction of PtdIns 4-kinase activity in fly cells. Zebrafish larvae also express pi4k2a, with its transcription altered by neonicotinoid insecticide exposure. More broadly, PI4KII is described as present across eukaryotic cells and organisms, reflecting its conserved and essential role in phosphatidylinositol 4-phosphate synthesis and intracellular membrane trafficking.

questions.body: How often is hypertension present in patients with Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: Hypertension is one of the most distinguishing features of Cushing's syndrome and is highly prevalent among affected patients. In adult patients with endogenous Cushing's syndrome, hypertension is present in approximately 80% of cases. In children and adolescents, the prevalence is somewhat lower, affecting about 47% to half of pediatric patients. The development of hypertension is significantly correlated with the duration of hypercortisolism and results from multiple pathophysiological mechanisms affecting plasma volume, peripheral vascular resistance, and cardiac output.

questions.body: Please list the FDA approved Janus kinase inhibitors
questions.ideal_answer: Based on the provided abstracts, the FDA-approved Janus kinase (JAK) inhibitors include baricitinib and ritlecitinib, which are approved for severe alopecia areata. Momelotinib and pacritinib are approved for myelofibrosis. Ruxolitinib cream is approved for mild to moderate atopic dermatitis and nonsegmental vitiligo. Upadacitinib is approved for moderate-to-severe Crohn's disease. Tofacitinib is approved for rheumatoid arthritis and is referenced in the context of postmarketing class warnings for JAK inhibitors. Delgocitinib 2% cream is approved for moderate-to-severe chronic hand eczema. These drugs span both oral and topical formulations and target various inflammatory and autoimmune conditions by inhibiting JAK signaling pathways.

questions.body: How does protein localization to lipid droplets occur?
questions.ideal_answer: Protein localization to lipid droplets occurs via two main pathways. The CYTOLD pathway involves cytosolic proteins binding the lipid droplet surface through amphipathic helices or hydrophobic domains. The ERTOLD pathway involves proteins originating from the endoplasmic reticulum, often bearing hydrophobic hairpin motifs, reaching lipid droplets during or after their formation via ER-LD membrane bridges. Additional mechanisms include lipid modifications and protein-protein interactions. The unique phospholipid monolayer surface of lipid droplets necessitates these specialized targeting strategies distinct from other organelles.

questions.body: How do glucocorticoid withdrawal syndrome's symptoms evolve over time after surgical remission of Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: Following surgical remission of Cushing's syndrome, glucocorticoid withdrawal syndrome symptoms appear immediately postoperatively, with myalgias, arthralgias, fatigue, weakness, sleep disturbances, and mood changes being most prevalent. Rather than improving early, most symptoms persist throughout the first 12 weeks, and myalgias, arthralgias, and weakness notably worsen during weeks 5 through 12. Physical quality of life scores decline while Cushing-specific quality of life improves, reflecting competing influences of withdrawal and recovery. Generally, GWS symptoms resolve as the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis recovers, though myopathy and neurocognitive dysfunction may persist as chronic complications.

questions.body: Relative to intestinal disease, what are HETE and HODE
questions.ideal_answer: HETE (hydroxyeicosatrienoic acids) and HODE (hydroxyoctadecadienoic acids) are biologically active lipid mediators produced through lipoxygenase-mediated metabolism of arachidonic acid and linoleic acid, respectively. In the context of intestinal disease, they function as inflammatory signaling molecules. In inflammatory bowel diseases, specific HETE metabolites such as 5-HETE, 12-HETE, 15-HETE, and 16RS-HETE correlate with disease activity, duration, and inflammatory markers like calprotectin, particularly in ulcerative colitis. Additionally, in iron-deficient intestinal states, induction of the enzyme Alox15 drives production of 12-HETE and 13-HODE, perturbing intestinal lipid homeostasis and potentially influencing villus structure and cell proliferation.

questions.body: Can proteins acquire new functions after forming amyloid structures?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, proteins can acquire new functions after forming amyloid structures. Multiple lines of evidence support this conclusion. Bacterial functional amyloids such as curli (CsgA) and FapC not only scaffold biofilms but also display intrinsic catalytic activity, a capability absent in their soluble monomeric forms. Amelogenin transitions into amyloid nanoribbons that template ordered apatite growth during enamel biomineralization, a function enabled specifically by the amyloid assembly state. Marine adhesive proteins form amyloid-like structures that confer strong adhesion properties suited for ecological roles. The reproductive cystatin CRES assembles into structurally heterogeneous amyloid forms that support host defense and sperm maturation in the epididymal matrix, with DNA binding modulating assembly pathways to achieve functional diversity. Additionally, stress-inducible amyloid bodies (A-bodies) in eukaryotic cells represent a functional amyloid state that induces protective cellular dormancy, a role not performed by the constituent proteins in their soluble state. Collectively, these examples demonstrate that the amyloid fold is not merely a structural endpoint but a platform that can confer novel biochemical activities, mechanical properties, and physiological roles upon proteins that adopt it.

questions.body: How often do patients fail to self-administer hydrocortisone injections in cases of acute adrenal crisis?
questions.ideal_answer: According to a 2022 survey of individuals with adrenal insufficiency conducted through the National Adrenal Diseases Foundation, nearly half (41%) of patients were unable to self-administer an emergency glucocorticoid injection during an acute adrenal crisis. The primary barriers cited were the effects of the crisis-associated illness itself and confusion experienced by the patients. Failed injections led to bad outcomes, including becoming sicker, requiring hospitalization, or death, in 36% of cases. These findings highlight that relying solely on patient self-injection may result in worse outcomes, and underscore the importance of patient education, practical instruction in injection technique, awareness of the potential need for assistance during a crisis, and the value of an approved glucocorticoid autoinjector.

questions.body: What is a trichoscope used for?
questions.ideal_answer: A trichoscope is a noninvasive diagnostic tool used for the diagnosis and follow-up of hair and scalp disorders. It allows recognition of morphologic structures not visible to the naked eye, enabling detailed examination of the hair and scalp. Trichoscopy is employed to evaluate causes of alopecia such as alopecia areata, tinea capitis, trichotillomania, and androgenetic alopecia, revealing distinct features like exclamation mark hairs, comma hairs, corkscrew hairs, yellow dots, white dots, and hair shaft diameter diversity. It facilitates rapid, noninvasive diagnosis of patchy hair loss in pediatric patients, enables early diagnosis and severity assessment of androgenetic alopecia, and can identify prognostic markers such as the 'i hair' sign in alopecia areata and trichotillomania. Overall, the routine use of trichoscopy enhances diagnostic capabilities beyond simple clinical inspection and contributes to improved patient management.
exact_answer: diagnosis and follow-up of hair and scalp disorders
detect more details of patchy hair loss
evaluate the causes of focal non-cicatricial alopecia

questions.body: How many kinases belong to the serine/arginine kinase family of the human kinome?
questions.ideal_answer: The serine/arginine protein kinase (SRPK) family of the human kinome consists of three members: SRPK1, SRPK2, and SRPK3. SRPK1 and SRPK2 are well-established members that phosphorylate serine-arginine rich (SR) proteins to regulate pre-mRNA alternative splicing, and both have been implicated in various cancers and viral infections such as hepatitis B. SRPK3 is the least studied member but has been implicated in breast cancer; the first selective chemical probes for SRPK3 have recently been described. All three kinases share a conserved catalytic mechanism targeting serine residues in RS domains of SR proteins, and their dysregulation is associated with oncogenesis and aberrant splicing.

questions.body: What are the subtypes of Cushing Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: Cushing Syndrome is broadly divided into ACTH-dependent and ACTH-independent forms. ACTH-dependent subtypes include Cushing's disease, caused by corticotroph pituitary tumors overproducing ACTH, and ectopic Cushing syndrome, resulting from ACTH overproduction by nonpituitary neuroendocrine tumors. ACTH-independent subtypes encompass adrenal Cushing syndrome, arising from autonomous cortisol production by adrenal adenomas, hyperplasia, or carcinomas. Additional recognized conditions include functional non-neoplastic hypercortisolism, formerly known as pseudo-Cushing state, mild autonomous cortisol secretion often associated with adrenal incidentalomas, and bilateral adrenal adenomas. Recent advances have broadened the differential diagnosis spectrum, with new genetic classifications of adrenal Cushing syndrome and increased recognition of milder forms, all of which guide individualized management strategies.

questions.body: What are the symptoms of TAFRO syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: TAFRO syndrome is a rare systemic inflammatory disorder whose name is an acronym derived from its defining symptoms. These include thrombocytopenia (low platelet count), anasarca (generalized edema or fluid retention throughout the body), fever, reticulin fibrosis (scarring or fibrosis in the bone marrow), renal dysfunction (kidney problems), and organomegaly (enlargement of organs such as the liver and spleen). The syndrome is associated with hypercytokinaemia and cytokine storms, and it overlaps clinically with idiopathic multicentric Castleman disease (iMCD), with which it is closely related. Non-specific symptoms may also be present, complicating diagnosis and management.

questions.body: What are the most common diseases caused by dysfunction of the ubiquitin system?
questions.ideal_answer: Dysfunction of the ubiquitin system is implicated in several major human diseases, particularly neurodegenerative disorders and cancer. OTU deubiquitinase dysregulation has been explicitly linked to cancer. Alzheimer's disease is associated with altered USP10 activity affecting tau pathology, as well as defects in MLKL-USP7-UBA52 signaling that impair ubiquitin homeostasis and autophagy. Parkinson's disease progression involves disruption of ubiquitin-mediated proteolysis pathways, with key biomarker genes such as LRRK2 implicated in ubiquitin-related processes. Huntington's disease is connected to USP11 dysregulation affecting mitochondrial function and neuronal survival, as well as impaired SUMyolation-mediated ubiquitylation and proteasomal degradation of mutant huntingtin. Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis is linked to dysfunction of UBQLN2, a ubiquitin-proteasome system receptor, and to increased UFMylation of myosin in skeletal muscle. Together, these findings underscore the broad pathophysiological impact of ubiquitin system perturbations across neurodegenerative diseases and cancer.

questions.body: What is the cortisol awakening response (CAR)?
questions.ideal_answer: The cortisol awakening response (CAR) is the rapid increase in cortisol levels that occurs within approximately 30 to 45 minutes after morning awakening. It represents a well-defined component of the broader cortisol secretory period surrounding waking and is frequently studied in stress research. In healthy individuals, the CAR is regulated by circadian, environmental, and neurocognitive processes. It is proposed to serve an adaptive function by mobilizing metabolic, immunological, and neurocognitive resources to prepare the organism for the challenges of the upcoming day and to help counterregulate adverse prior-day emotional experiences.

questions.body: What  type of  blood cell has  CD19 as a surface biomarker?
questions.ideal_answer: CD19 is a surface biomarker specifically expressed on B cells. It is a transmembrane glycoprotein, also known as the B-lymphocyte antigen, that serves as a B cell-specific surface marker expressed on the majority of neoplastic B cells. Studies investigating peroxisome biogenesis in human peripheral blood mononuclear cells specifically analyzed CD19+ B cells, distinguishing them from CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, and CD14+ monocytes. Additionally, CD19 has emerged as an attractive biomarker and therapeutic target for B-cell malignancies, and aptamers have been developed to target its outer membrane domain for use in B-cell malignancy-targeted therapies.

questions.body: What are the most common vectors used in gene therapy?
questions.ideal_answer: The most common vectors used in gene therapy fall into two broad categories: viral and non-viral. Among viral vectors, adeno-associated viruses (AAV) are currently the leading platform, accounting for the majority of recent FDA-approved gene therapies due to their safety profile and efficiency across hematologic, neuromuscular, dermatologic, and neurogenetic diseases. Lentiviruses are also widely used, particularly when larger gene inserts or semipermanent expression are needed, such as in neural and oncologic applications. Adenoviruses and retroviruses are prevalent in clinical settings, while herpes simplex virus and baculoviral vectors are explored in cancer trials and specific applications like myocardial infarction therapy, respectively. Non-viral delivery systems are an important and growing alternative, including liposomes and related technologies such as solid lipid nanoparticles, polymer nanoparticles, dendrimers, polymeric micelles, and polymeric nanoparticles. These non-viral systems offer enhanced loading capacities and can deliver nucleic acids, mRNA, or proteins directly into cells, though they require continued optimization to address immunogenicity and toxicity concerns.

questions.body: What is the time to diagnosis for adrenal Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: According to a meta-analysis based on 5367 patients, the mean time to diagnosis for adrenal Cushing's Syndrome was 30 months. This finding comes from a systematic literature search that included 44 studies examining the time span between first symptoms and diagnosis across different subtypes of Cushing's Syndrome. The overall mean time to diagnosis across all subtypes was 34 months, with notable variation by subtype: ectopic CS had the shortest mean time to diagnosis at 14 months, adrenal CS was intermediate at 30 months, and pituitary CS had the longest mean time to diagnosis at 38 months. No significant differences in time to diagnosis were found based on gender, age (under 18 versus 18 years or older), or year of diagnosis (before versus after 2000). The authors concluded that time to diagnosis remains unacceptably long and requires improvement, as early diagnosis is important for prevention of complications given that signs and symptoms of Cushing's Syndrome overlap with common conditions such as metabolic syndrome, obesity, osteoporosis, and depression.

questions.body: What is the indication for Brineura?
questions.ideal_answer: Brineura (cerliponase alfa) is indicated to slow the loss of ambulation in pediatric patients with neuronal ceroid lipofuscinosis type 2 (CLN2), a pediatric neurodegenerative lysosomal storage disorder caused by a deficiency in tripeptidyl peptidase-1 (TPP1). It was first approved in the USA for the treatment of motor function loss in pediatric patients aged 3 years and older with CLN2, and subsequently received approval in the EU for CLN2 in patients of all ages. Administered via intracerebroventricular infusion every two weeks, cerliponase alfa has been shown to reduce the progression of functional decline and slow the progression of milestone deterioration in CLN2.
exact_answer: to slow the loss of ambulation in pediatric patients with CLN2
treatment of motor function loss in paediatric patients ≥3 years of age with CLN2

questions.body: What is the primary energy source for cancer cells?
questions.ideal_answer: Glucose is the primary energy source for cancer cells. Cancer cells exhibit metabolic reprogramming characterized by preferential aerobic glycolysis, known as the Warburg effect, whereby they consume glucose and produce lactate even in the presence of oxygen. This less efficient but rapid means of energy generation supports tumor cell proliferation, migration, and survival. Glutamine is recognized as the second most utilized energy source after glucose, supporting cancer cell growth through glutaminolysis. Cancer cells also display metabolic plasticity, allowing them to utilize lipids and amino acids as alternative substrates. In glucose-poor environments, lactate produced as a byproduct of glycolysis can be repurposed as an alternative energy source via monocarboxylate transporters such as MCT1. The importance of glucose is further underscored by studies showing that glucose transporter upregulation (e.g., GLUT1) and signaling pathways such as WNT7B/β-catenin and NF-κB promote glucose uptake and glycolysis in cancer cells, while glucose oxidase-based therapeutic strategies exploit the cancer cell dependence on glucose as a metabolic vulnerability.

questions.body: Are adrenocortical secretory tumors in humans mostly a hereditary disorder?
questions.ideal_answer: Adrenocortical secretory tumors in humans are not mostly a hereditary disorder. According to the abstract, while these tumors can occasionally belong to hereditary syndromes that predispose individuals to tumor development, the majority of secreting adrenocortical tumors are sporadic in nature, meaning they arise randomly without an inherited genetic basis. These tumors are also described as rare overall, and when they do secrete steroids, they can cause conditions such as Cushing syndrome, primary aldosteronism, virilization, or feminization. Most are benign adenomas, though some can be malignant carcinomas with a poor prognosis. Treatment typically involves surgery, often performed laparoscopically for benign tumors. The key point is that the sporadic origin of these tumors is explicitly stated in the literature, making hereditary predisposition the exception rather than the rule.

questions.body: Please describe alopecia areata.
questions.ideal_answer: Alopecia areata is an autoimmune disease that causes nonscarring hair loss. It is characterized by an inflammatory infiltrate targeting hair follicle bulbs, primarily composed of lymphocytes, plasma cells, and other immune cells, a condition known as bulbitis. Clinically, it presents with patchy alopecia and sometimes leukotrichia, typically without erythema or crusting. The disease can be active, inactive, or in a regrowth phase. It is a chronic, relapsing condition that often requires long-term treatment, and diagnosis is supported by trichoscopy and histopathology.

questions.body: What is the intracellular localization of PI4KIIalpha in human cells?
questions.ideal_answer: PI4KIIalpha exhibits diverse intracellular localization in human cells. It is predominantly found at the trans-Golgi network (TGN) and Golgi complex, including early Golgi compartments, and is detected in juxtanuclear membrane pools and perinuclear regions. PI4KIIalpha also localizes to synaptic vesicles and synapses in neuronal cells, as well as to exocytotic vesicles in mast cells. It is present on endosomes, specifically LAMP-1-positive endosomes, and within AP-3-positive organelles, including both perinuclear and peripheral organelles and nerve terminals. Its tight membrane association, mediated by palmitoylation, targets it to the TGN and lipid rafts. Under certain conditions it can also be detected at the cellular surface.

questions.body: What is the prevalence of secondary adrenal insufficiency?
questions.ideal_answer: The prevalence of secondary adrenal insufficiency is estimated to be between 150-280 per million people. The most frequent cause is believed to be corticosteroid-induced insufficiency, although the clinical relevance of deficiency after cessation of glucocorticoid treatment is widely debated. For comparison, primary adrenal insufficiency has a lower estimated prevalence of 82-144 per million, with autoimmunity being the most common cause in adults and genetic causes in children.

questions.body: SynNeurGe classification system is associated with what disease?
questions.ideal_answer: The SynNeurGe classification system is associated with Parkinson's disease. It is a biologically based classification proposed to address the complexity and heterogeneity of Parkinson's disease by integrating three components: presence or absence of pathological α-synuclein in tissues or CSF (S), evidence of underlying neurodegeneration defined by neuroimaging procedures (N), and documentation of pathogenic gene variants that cause or strongly predispose to Parkinson's disease (G). These three components are linked to a clinical component (C) defined by high-specificity or multiple lower-specificity clinical features. The system aims to facilitate both basic and clinical research and move the field toward precision medicine and disease-modifying therapies. Its initial application is recommended exclusively for research settings, pending prospective validation and consideration of ethical implications.

questions.body: How is the pathogenic prion protein formed from the normal prion protein?
questions.ideal_answer: The pathogenic prion protein (PrPSc) is formed from the normal cellular prion protein (PrPC) through a conformational conversion, where PrPC undergoes a structural change from its normal folded state to an abnormally misfolded form. In the favored model, PrPSc acts as a template that directly interacts with endogenous PrPC, inducing it to adopt the pathogenic conformation and thereby propagating the misfolding in a chain reaction. Factors such as trace metals like copper and specific point mutations, such as E200K, can promote this conversion by destabilizing normal protein structure through loss of key salt-bridge interactions and increased molecular motion, facilitating the transition from PrPC to PrPSc.

questions.body: What is the average delay in the diagnosis of Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: The average delay in the diagnosis of Cushing's Syndrome is approximately 34 months (about 2 years and 10 months), based on a meta-analysis of 5367 patients. This delay varies by subtype: ectopic CS has the shortest time to diagnosis at 14 months, adrenal CS at 30 months, and pituitary CS the longest at 38 months. No significant differences were found based on gender, age, or year of diagnosis. A separate cohort of patients undergoing adrenalectomy reported a median time to diagnosis of 24 months. Patient survey data indicate that 49% of patients experienced a diagnostic delay exceeding 2 years. The European Registry on Cushing's Syndrome (ERCUSYN), encompassing 1564 patients, similarly reported a delay of approximately 2 years between symptom onset and diagnosis, with many specialists consulted who often missed the correct diagnosis. These delays are largely attributed to the overlap of Cushing's Syndrome symptoms with common conditions such as metabolic syndrome, obesity, osteoporosis, and depression, underscoring the need for improved diagnostic strategies to reduce the burden of disease.

questions.body: Which of the 5 senses does otospongiosis affect?
questions.ideal_answer: Otospongiosis, also known as otosclerosis, affects the sense of hearing. It is a primary osteodystrophy of the otic capsule of the inner ear and one of the leading causes of deafness in adults. The condition commonly causes conductive hearing loss due to fixation of the stapes footplate, but can also present with sensorineural or mixed hearing loss when the cochlea is involved. Medical treatments such as sodium fluoride and bisphosphonates aim to stabilize hearing thresholds and slow disease progression, while surgical interventions such as stapedotomy are performed to improve hearing outcomes by closing the air-bone gap.

questions.body: How is terminal differentiation regulated?
questions.ideal_answer: Terminal differentiation is regulated through multiple coordinated mechanisms. The DREAM complex represses cell cycle gene expression to initiate G0 entry, while retinoblastoma protein (RB) suppresses E2F transcription factors to maintain cell cycle exit. Chromatin accessibility changes at enhancers of key cell cycle regulators such as CyclinE, E2F1, and String enforce robust, permanent cell cycle arrest independently of cycling status. Proneural transcription factors including Ascl1, NGN2, and SOX11 drive differentiation commitment, which occurs simultaneously with cell cycle exit in G1. Heterochromatin clustering further stabilizes gene repression as a downstream consequence of cell cycle exit. The cilium-centrosome axis also participates in distinguishing reversible quiescence from terminal differentiation, collectively ensuring that terminally differentiated cells become permanently refractory to mitogenic signals.

questions.body: What are the common symptoms of adrenal insufficiency?
questions.ideal_answer: Common symptoms of adrenal insufficiency include general weakness, fatigue, anorexia, arthralgia, fever, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, myalgias, weight loss, and salt craving. Laboratory findings frequently include hyponatremia and hyperkalemia. Without treatment, patients may develop hyperpigmented skin and mucus membranes. Acute adrenal crisis, a medical emergency, is characterized by hypotension, lethargy, tachycardia, nausea, vomiting, and hypoglycemia, requiring prompt intervention with intravenous fluids and stress-dose hydrocortisone.

questions.body: Krabbe disease, also known as globoid-cell leukodystrophy is thought to results from what toxic metabolite?
questions.ideal_answer: Krabbe disease, also known as globoid-cell leukodystrophy, is caused by a functional deficiency of the lysosomal enzyme galactocerebrosidase (GALC), also referred to as galactosylceramidase. This deficiency leads to the progressive accumulation of the toxic metabolite psychosine. Psychosine accumulation affects many different cellular pathways, leading to severe demyelination of both the central and peripheral nervous systems. Additionally, psychosine has been shown to potentiate inflammatory responses, including increased TNF-alpha production by peripheral blood mononuclear cells in Krabbe disease patients, further contributing to the pathogenesis of the disease.

questions.body: How do cancer cells attack the cells of the immune system?
questions.ideal_answer: Cancer cells attack immune cells through multiple mechanisms. They upregulate PD-L1 to bind PD-1 on T cells, triggering apoptosis and anergy. They release exosomes carrying immunosuppressive molecules like miR-24-3p to directly induce T-cell apoptosis. Cancer cells also secrete proteins such as galectin-4, which binds CD3 receptors on T cells causing apoptosis, and des-γ-carboxy prothrombin, which elevates reactive oxygen species in T cells. Additionally, tumors polarize macrophages toward an M2 phenotype that secretes CCL18, further promoting CD8+ T-cell apoptosis. Regulatory T-cell differentiation and T-cell exhaustion are also induced through soluble factors in the tumor microenvironment, collectively dismantling effective anti-tumor immunity.

questions.body: When and how does an Addisonian crisis manifest?
questions.ideal_answer: An Addisonian crisis occurs when hydrocortisone demand is not met during physiological stress, such as infection, fever, trauma, surgery, or acute psychological stress, particularly in patients with known or undiagnosed adrenal insufficiency. It can develop within hours from apparent good health. Clinically, it manifests as severe hypotension and shock unresponsive to fluids or vasopressors, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia, altered mental status, lethargy, nausea, vomiting, and tachycardia. If untreated, it can progress to cardiovascular collapse and death, making prompt recognition and immediate treatment with parenteral hydrocortisone essential.

questions.body: Is Calcium dyshomeostasis  assoiciated with Huntington's disease?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, calcium dyshomeostasis is associated with Huntington's disease (HD). Multiple lines of evidence support this connection. The huntingtin gene mutation, caused by expanded CAG repeats, leads to disturbances in neuronal calcium homeostasis that are considered important factors linking the mutation to the onset and progression of HD. Mitochondrial calcium dyshomeostasis is specifically implicated in the pathogenesis of the disorder. HD is also characterized by a positive feedback loop between calcium dyshomeostasis and the aggregation of the huntingtin protein, which escalates over time and ultimately leads to neuronal loss. The calcium dyshomeostasis in HD encompasses mitochondrial and endoplasmic reticulum dysfunction, impaired calcium buffering, glutamate excitotoxicity, and alterations in calcium entry routes into neurons. Consensus is gradually emerging that disturbances in calcium homeostasis, together with mitochondrial stress, are key players in the molecular pathogenesis of Huntington's disease.

questions.body: What are the most common mechanisms leading to amyloid formation?
questions.ideal_answer: Amyloid formation arises through several interconnected mechanisms. Molecular interactions, particularly charge-charge interactions and salting-out effects, can accelerate aggregation by stabilizing compact conformers through intra- and intermolecular forces. Disruption of the supersaturated state above thermodynamic solubility, for example by molecules like ATP, can trigger amyloid nucleation. Conformational transitions, such as shifts from unfolded monomeric states to molten globule-like intermediates, predispose proteins to aggregation. An imbalance between in vivo amyloid production and natural clearance drives progressive deposition in diseases like transthyretin cardiomyopathy. Protein misfolding and aggregation are fundamental processes, influenced by genetic mutations and sequence variations such as the isoleucine-for-valine SNP in LECT2. Membrane protein dimerization, as seen with the amyloid precursor protein APP, regulates amyloid peptide production and subsequent fibril or oligomer formation. Metabolic stress can also accelerate amyloid formation, as demonstrated by IAPP accumulation in pancreatic beta cells. Together, these mechanisms operate within a cellular context shaped by chaperone activity, proteostasis network capacity, and the intrinsic structural properties of amyloidogenic proteins.
exact_answer: conformational transitions from the unfolded monomeric state to a molten globule-like state
disrupting the supersaturated state above the thermodynamic solubility
imbalance between in vivo amyloid production and natural clearance

questions.body: How is acute adrenal crisis treated?
questions.ideal_answer: Acute adrenal crisis is treated with prompt administration of parenteral hydrocortisone at stress doses (typically 100 mg intramuscularly or intravenously), which may be 10 to 15 times the physiological dose. Intravenous fluid resuscitation is essential to address dehydration and hypotension. Electrolyte abnormalities such as hyponatremia and hyperkalemia, as well as hypoglycemia, must be identified and corrected. Blood pressure support may also be required. If intravenous access cannot be quickly established, intramuscular administration should be performed without delay. Early recognition and rapid intervention are critical to prevent progression to shock, multiorgan failure, and death.

questions.body: is Paracoccidioidomycosis caused by a parasite?
questions.ideal_answer: Paracoccidioidomycosis is not caused by a parasite. All three abstracts consistently identify it as a systemic mycosis, meaning it is a fungal disease. The causative agent is the dimorphic fungus Paracoccidioides brasiliensis and related species (Paracoccidioides spp.), which are endemic to Latin America. Abstract 1 describes it as a neglected tropical fungal disease, Abstract 2 documents a case in which Paracoccidioides brasiliensis was cultured and identified as the pathogen, and Abstract 3 explicitly states it is caused by the dimorphic fungus Paracoccidioides brasiliensis and related species. Fungi and parasites are distinct categories of organisms; fungi belong to the kingdom Fungi, whereas parasites are typically protozoa, helminths, or ectoparasites. Therefore, Paracoccidioidomycosis is definitively fungal in etiology, not parasitic.

questions.body: What are the most common bacteria in the human skin microbiome?
questions.ideal_answer: The human skin microbiome is dominated by several bacterial genera and species. Staphylococcus is the most consistently mentioned genus, with Staphylococcus epidermidis being the ubiquitous commensal; other notable species include S. capitis, S. hominis, and S. warneri. Streptococcus species, particularly S. mitis and S. parasanguinis, are also found in high abundance. Corynebacterium is another major component, with C. tuberculostearicum being the most prevalent species and newly identified species such as C. axilliensis, C. jamesii, and C. bovis also described. Micrococcus, including M. luteus, and Kocuria are regularly identified as core skin microbiome members. Cutibacterium, particularly C. acnes, is a dominant genus especially on sebaceous skin sites. Neisseriaceae has also been identified as a predominant family on facial skin. Together, these bacteria represent the core microbial community of healthy human skin.

questions.body: What is the mean time to diagnosis for ectopic Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: According to a meta-analysis based on 5367 patients, the mean time to diagnosis for ectopic Cushing's Syndrome was 14 months. This was the shortest among the subtypes of Cushing's Syndrome, compared to adrenal Cushing's Syndrome at 30 months and pituitary Cushing's Syndrome at 38 months. The overall mean time to diagnosis across all subtypes was 34 months. No significant differences in time to diagnosis were found based on gender, age (under or over 18 years), or year of diagnosis (before or after 2000).

questions.body: What is the realtionship between survivin and nasopharyngeal cancer?
questions.ideal_answer: Survivin is significantly overexpressed in nasopharyngeal carcinoma at both the mRNA and protein levels compared to normal nasopharyngeal tissue. Its overexpression is associated with poor overall survival, lymph node metastasis, distant metastasis, local recurrence, and advanced clinical staging. Survivin functions as an apoptosis inhibitor that promotes tumor cell proliferation, and its suppression inhibits NPC cell growth. It also correlates positively with EBV infection markers and co-expressed oncogenic proteins like β-catenin and TCF-4. Additionally, survivin autoantibodies serve as diagnostic biomarkers for NPC detection. Overall, survivin acts as both a prognostic indicator and a potential therapeutic target in nasopharyngeal carcinoma.

questions.body: Is the inactive X chromosome (Barr body) less susceptible to genotoxic damage or mutations than the active X chromosome?
questions.ideal_answer: The inactive X chromosome (Xi) is not less susceptible to genotoxic damage or mutations than the active X chromosome. Studies show that both chromosomes exhibit similar radiosensitivity, meaning a comparable number of breaks are induced in each following irradiation (Abstract 8). While repair hierarchies exist—with inactive genes generally repaired more slowly than active genes (Abstracts 6 and 7)—this reflects differences in repair efficiency rather than initial susceptibility to damage. In fact, slower repair on the Xi could lead to greater accumulation of mutations, not fewer. The Xi also participates actively in DNA damage responses: chromatin relaxation and repair factor recruitment occur at the Xi following double-strand breaks (Abstract 3), and DNA damage can disrupt the epigenetic state of the Xi, such as reducing H3K27me3 deposition (Abstract 4). Furthermore, Abstract 8 found that repair inhibition actually increased aberrations involving the inactive X, suggesting its repair is less efficient rather than its damage being less frequent. Collectively, the evidence does not support the notion that the inactive X chromosome is inherently less susceptible to genotoxic damage or mutations compared to the active X chromosome.

questions.body: What are the causes of the different categories of adrenal insufficiency?
questions.ideal_answer: Primary adrenal insufficiency results from direct damage or dysfunction of the adrenal gland, caused by autoimmune destruction, congenital adrenal hyperplasia, infections such as tuberculosis or fungal disease, pharmacological inhibition by agents like azole antifungals, or surgical removal. Secondary adrenal insufficiency stems from pituitary or hypothalamic disorders that reduce ACTH or corticotropin-releasing hormone production, including tumors, hemorrhage, inflammatory conditions, surgery, radiation, or opioid use. Glucocorticoid-induced adrenal insufficiency occurs when prolonged administration of supraphysiological doses of exogenous glucocorticoids suppresses the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis, leading to cortisol deficiency upon tapering or discontinuation.

questions.body: Please list the common side effects of Kymriah
questions.ideal_answer: Kymriah (tisagenlecleucel), an anti-CD19 CAR T-cell therapy approved for relapsed/refractory B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia, is associated with several notable side effects. The most prominently described toxicities in the provided context are cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and CAR T-cell-related encephalopathy syndrome (CRES), both of which can be severe or even fatal, underscoring the importance of careful management. Additional side effects associated with Kymriah include hypogammaglobulinemia, infections, prolonged cytopenias, neurological events, and encephalopathy. These toxicities reflect the powerful immune activation that CAR T-cell therapy induces and highlight the need for close monitoring and proactive management strategies in patients receiving this treatment.

questions.body: Which factors trigger eustress responses in the body?
questions.ideal_answer: Eustress responses in the body are triggered by a range of factors. Regular physical activity and exercise generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) through mitochondrial and NADPH oxidase systems, which serve as key modulators of redox signaling and adaptive responses. Environmental enrichment (EE), providing enhanced sensory, cognitive, motor, and social stimulation, induces eustress through hormetic pathways that promote neuroplasticity and stress resilience. In humans, specific EE-analogous activities such as physical exercise, cognitive challenges, social engagement, and music function as mild stressors triggering beneficial adaptation. Preconditioning with small doses of stressful stimuli mobilizes antioxidant defense mechanisms, representing a classic hormetic or eustress response. The phytochemical luteolin can activate Nrf2 signaling, contributing to cellular redox balance and oxidative eustress. Enriched environments also upregulate beneficial gut microbiota, particularly Lactobacillus spp., which mediate systemic anti-tumor immunity. Together, these factors illustrate that eustress is induced by mild physical, chemical, environmental, and psychosocial stimuli that promote adaptive, health-enhancing responses.

questions.body: Is Cushing Syndrome present in canine populations?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, Cushing's syndrome (naturally occurring hypercortisolism) is well established in canine populations. It is described as a common endocrine disorder in dogs, caused in 80-85% of cases by an ACTH-producing pituitary adenoma (pituitary-dependent hypercortisolism) and in 15-20% of cases by a cortisol-secreting adrenocortical tumor. Epidemiological data from UK primary-care practices estimate a 1-year period prevalence of 0.17% and an annual incidence of 0.06% among dogs under veterinary care. Certain breeds carry higher risk, including Bichon frises, Border terriers, and Miniature schnauzers, while Golden retrievers and Labrador retrievers appear more protected. Increasing age and body weight above the breed-sex mean are also associated with higher odds of diagnosis. Diagnosis can be challenging due to variable clinical presentation, and machine learning tools using routine laboratory data have been developed to assist. Treatment options include surgical removal of the causative tumor, radiotherapy, and pharmacotherapy with drugs such as trilostane and mitotane, with ongoing research into more targeted therapies.

questions.body: Does a zoonosis affect humans?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, zoonoses affect humans by definition, as they are diseases transmitted from animals to humans. The provided abstracts offer multiple examples. Western equine encephalitis is a zoonotic disease in which humans are accidental hosts, and it can cause serious neurological conditions such as meningoencephalitis, particularly in children. Spotted fever group rickettsioses, caused by pathogens like Rickettsia heilongjiangensis, pose a significant zoonotic threat to public health. Tularemia, caused by Francisella tularensis, is a nationally notifiable zoonosis in the United States that caused 2,462 reported cases between 2011 and 2022, with clinical manifestations including fever and localized symptoms and a case fatality rate that can exceed 2% depending on the strain and clinical form. Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever is a tick-borne zoonotic disease present in many African countries, with serological evidence of human exposure documented in northern Tanzania, where exposure was closely linked to livestock activities such as milking. Together, these examples confirm that zoonoses can and do affect humans, sometimes causing severe or fatal illness.

questions.body: How do larval salivary glands differ from adult salivary glands in Drosophila melanogaster?
questions.ideal_answer: Larval salivary glands in Drosophila melanogaster are transient epithelial tubular organs that function in exocrine secretion of saliva and mucoprotein glue for pupal attachment, and also act as endocrine organs secreting peptides like Sgsf to regulate systemic growth. They are histolyzed during metamorphosis through programmed cell death involving autophagy and apoptosis triggered by ecdysone signaling. Adult salivary glands, by contrast, develop from imaginal tissues during metamorphosis and persist in the adult fly. Thus, larval and adult salivary glands differ fundamentally in developmental origin, lifespan, and ultimate fate.

questions.body: How often is leukocytosis present in patients with Cushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: Leukocytosis is frequently observed in patients with Cushing's Syndrome. A study of 26 patients with Cushing's disease found elevated white blood cell counts in approximately 40% of cases (11/28 events), with mean baseline WBC of 10,500 cells/µl dropping significantly after treatment. A larger nationwide retrospective cohort study of 297 CS patients reported leukocytosis in 21.5% of patients with Cushing's Syndrome compared to 8.9% in matched controls, with significantly higher WBC, neutrophil counts, and neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratios in CS patients regardless of pituitary or adrenal source. Both studies confirmed that white blood cell counts decrease following successful treatment or remission, though levels may remain elevated compared to controls even post-remission. Notably, baseline leukocytosis in Cushing's disease patients was associated with a lower remission rate (36.7% vs. 63.9% in those with normal WBC), suggesting it may serve as a prognostic marker.

questions.body: What are the hallmarks of Acro-cardio-facial syndrome (ACFS)
questions.ideal_answer: Acro-cardio-facial syndrome (ACFS) is a rare genetic disorder characterized by a constellation of congenital anomalies. The core hallmarks include split-hand/split-foot malformation (ectrodactyly), cleft lip and/or palate, congenital heart defects (CHD), facial anomalies, and genital anomalies. Additional consistent features include intellectual disability (mental retardation) and growth retardation. Less frequently reported findings that may expand the phenotypic spectrum include mild scoliosis, hemivertebrae, and subclinical hyperthyroidism. An autosomal recessive inheritance pattern has been suggested based on parental consanguinity and recurrence in siblings, with some evidence pointing to a microdeletion in the 6q21-q22.3 chromosomal region as a possible cause in certain cases. Prognosis is generally poor, with many affected individuals not surviving the first days or months of life.

questions.body: What are the effects of D-amino acids on human health?
questions.ideal_answer: D-amino acids exert diverse effects on human health. They modulate glutamatergic neurotransmission via NMDA receptors, with D-serine, D-aspartate, and D-alanine implicated in schizophrenia pathophysiology, where reduced levels are observed in patients. They offer neuroprotection against excitotoxicity in neurodegenerative diseases. D-amino acid oxidase suppresses hepatocellular carcinoma growth by generating hydrogen peroxide. D-serine and D-asparagine serve as reliable biomarkers for renal function assessment in chronic kidney disease. Dietary D-amino acids influence lipid metabolism, neurotransmission, and immune regulation through glycerophospholipid and choline metabolism pathways. Additionally, D-amino acids exhibit antimicrobial and antibiofilm properties against pathogens, offering potential therapeutic strategies against drug-resistant infections.

questions.body: What is the mean time to diagnosis for pituitary Chushing's Syndrome?
questions.ideal_answer: According to a meta-analysis based on 5367 patients, the mean time to diagnosis for pituitary Cushing's Syndrome was 38 months. This is longer than the mean time to diagnosis for adrenal CS (30 months) and ectopic CS (14 months). The overall mean time to diagnosis across all subtypes of Cushing's Syndrome was 34 months. No significant differences in time to diagnosis were found based on gender, age (under 18 versus 18 years or older), or year of diagnosis (before versus after 2000). Notably, patients with pituitary CS had a longer time to diagnosis in Germany than elsewhere. These findings highlight that time to diagnosis remains unacceptably long and requires improvement, given that early diagnosis is important for prevention of complications associated with Cushing's Syndrome.

questions.body: Has Vitamin D therapy been shown to improve cognitive function in Alzheimers disease?
questions.ideal_answer: While observational studies suggest an association between higher vitamin D levels and better cognitive performance, and lower vitamin D levels have been found in Alzheimer's disease patients compared to those without dementia, rigorous evidence that vitamin D therapy actually improves cognitive function in Alzheimer's disease is lacking. A two-sample Mendelian randomization study, designed to minimize confounding bias, found no clear evidence that increased 25(OH)D concentrations causally improve cognitive performance in European ancestry individuals, including those over age 60. Although experimental and mechanistic studies suggest that the active form of vitamin D (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3) may exert anti-inflammatory effects in brain pericytes and modulate various pathways relevant to Alzheimer's disease, these findings do not constitute clinical proof of cognitive benefit. The observational associations likely reflect confounding rather than a true causal relationship. In summary, while vitamin D deficiency is associated with Alzheimer's disease and there are plausible biological mechanisms for benefit, current evidence does not support the conclusion that vitamin D therapy reliably improves cognitive function in Alzheimer's disease patients.

questions.body: Can AlphaFold be used in a reverse manner to predict a possible amino acid sequence from a desired protein structure (inverse protein folding)?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, AlphaFold can be used in a reverse manner to support inverse protein folding, the process of predicting amino acid sequences that fold into a desired protein structure. While AlphaFold itself is a forward folding model that predicts structure from sequence, its capabilities have been harnessed for inverse design in several ways. One key approach involves knowledge distillation of AlphaFold's confidence metrics, such as pTM or pLDDT scores, to create faster, end-to-end differentiable models that serve as structure consistency regularizers during training of inverse folding models, yielding improvements in sequence recovery and protein diversity. Additionally, AlphaFold's predicted structures can be used as input for inverse folding algorithms, expanding the structural coverage beyond experimentally resolved structures. Tools like SeqPredNN have also been developed to directly address the inverse folding problem, generating amino acid sequences expected to fold into specified tertiary structures, with AlphaFold used to validate the predicted folds. These approaches collectively demonstrate that AlphaFold's structural prediction framework, while not natively designed for inverse folding, plays a central role in enabling protein sequence design from target structures.

questions.body: Is Cushing syndrome present in feline populations?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, Cushing's syndrome (hyperadrenocorticism) is present in feline populations. Although it is diagnosed much less frequently in cats than in dogs, it is a well-recognized feline adrenal disorder with an incidence in cats probably similar to that in humans. Clinical signs of feline hyperadrenocorticism include some features unique to cats, such as unregulated diabetes mellitus and severe skin atrophy, as well as signs shared with dogs, including a potbellied appearance, polydipsia, polyuria, and increased susceptibility to infections. Clinicians must be familiar with the distinct clinical presentation and diagnostic testing approaches used in cats, as these differ from those employed in dogs.

questions.body: Is Heck's disease caused by a bacterium?
questions.ideal_answer: Heck's disease is not caused by a bacterium. According to the provided abstract, Heck's disease, also known as focal epithelial hyperplasia (FEH), is a condition caused by the human papillomavirus (HPV), which induces proliferation of epithelial cells resulting in asymptomatic lesions in the oral mucosa. The disease is more prevalent among children and within families, and while the lesions are generally self-limiting, they may progress or cause aesthetic and functional discomfort. There is no bacterial involvement in its etiology.

questions.body: Is situs inversus totalis compatible with life?
questions.ideal_answer: Yes, situs inversus totalis (SIT) is compatible with life. Multiple sources confirm that patients with SIT do not have a decreased survival rate compared to the general population and can live normal lives. SIT is a rare congenital anomaly occurring in approximately 1 in 8,000 to 1 in 25,000 live births, involving complete mirror-image reversal of thoracic and abdominal organs, but it generally carries no pathophysiological significance on its own. It is frequently diagnosed incidentally when patients present for other medical concerns, as illustrated by cases of a 93-year-old woman with SIT who survived colonic perforation surgery, a 34-year-old diabetic woman, and patients undergoing cancer surgeries. While SIT can be associated with conditions such as primary ciliary dyskinesia, which may complicate the clinical picture, the anatomical variation itself does not inherently shorten lifespan. Clinicians should be aware of SIT because it can present diagnostic and surgical challenges due to reversed anatomy, requiring careful radiological evaluation and planning before invasive procedures.

questions.body: What is the treatment of choice for Cushing's Disease?
questions.ideal_answer: The treatment of choice for Cushing's disease (CD) is trans-sphenoidal surgery (TSS). Although TSS is the preferred initial treatment, it is not always curative and is associated with a substantial rate of recurrence, with published recurrence rates varying between 5 and 20%. Approximately half of these recurrences appear within 5 years and the remaining half within or even after 10 years post-surgery. A low or undetectable cortisol in the immediate post-operative period is regarded as the best criterion of remission, though low post-op cortisol levels do not accurately predict long-term remission. Current guidelines recommend that every patient in remission be monitored for the possibility of recurrence using methods such as late night salivary cortisol, dexamethasone suppression test, and urinary free cortisol measurements.

questions.body: Why do men die earlier than women?
questions.ideal_answer: Men die earlier than women due to multiple interacting biological and behavioral factors. Men exhibit faster telomere shortening, indicating accelerated biological aging compared to women of the same chronological age. Testosterone is associated with poorer survival, while estrogens appear protective. Sex-specific differences in lipid metabolism and nutrient-sensing pathways also favor female longevity. Additionally, higher external mortality among younger men and greater smoking-related and cardiovascular disease mortality among older men significantly reduce male life expectancy. Women also show greater physiological plasticity for life extension under dietary restriction, and higher embryonic telomerase activity may establish longer telomeres in females from birth onward.

questions.body: What is the difference between Cushing Syndrome and Cushing Disease?
questions.ideal_answer: Cushing's syndrome is the general term for sustained pathologic hypercortisolism, which can result from excessive corticotropin (ACTH) secretion by tumors in the pituitary gland, ectopic ACTH-secreting tumors elsewhere, or ACTH-independent cortisol secretion directly from adrenal tumors. Cushing's disease is a specific subtype of Cushing's syndrome in which the hypercortisolism is caused by excess ACTH secretion from a tumor located in the pituitary gland, accounting for approximately 70% of all Cushing's syndrome cases. The remaining cases of Cushing's syndrome include ectopic ACTH secretion from non-pituitary tumors (about 15%) and ACTH-independent cortisol secretion from adrenal tumors (about 15%). Differentiating a pituitary source of excess ACTH (Cushing's disease) from an ectopic source is accomplished by imaging the pituitary and sampling for ACTH in the venous drainage of the pituitary. The prognosis is generally better for Cushing's disease than for adrenocortical cancer and malignant ACTH-producing tumors.
exact_answer: Cushing's disease is a specific subtype of Cushing's syndrome caused by excess ACTH secretion from a pituitary tumor
Cushing's syndrome is the broader condition of sustained pathologic hypercortisolism from any cause
Cushing's disease accounts for approximately 70% of Cushing's syndrome cases
